channel.title: Feedback on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Feedback on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Toxic Positivity
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about toxic positivity and I really want to talk about this.

I put it off for a while. Seems weird to be talking about it now, with all the negativity in the world, but this channel is almost three years old and there’s always been a bunch of negativity in the world and it doesn’t seem to be going away. And I think this topic is important and I’m going to say the word. I think it’s very nuanced, which is why I kept putting it off. I know if I start talking about toxic positivity, people are going to think I’m talking about a particular game or developer. Publisher. Can’t stop that.

item.title: Handling Advice
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about handling advice.

This is when people tell you how to do something. People love to dispense advice, by the way.

Now, I’m not talking about criticism. Criticism is when people want to talk about what you did, whether it’s a method you used in your development of a game, say, or they want to talk about the game itself, what they think about the game. That’s criticism.

item.title: Collecting Feedback
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about collecting feedback, specifically how and when and what to do with the feedback that you get on the game you’re making.

So this is obviously aimed at game developers, but I think I thought a lot of people would like hearing about this. So here we go. I’m going to break this into feedback you get before you ship and feedback you get after you ship, so before you ship, they’re basically three sources of feedback. You can get feedback from the team itself. People will be playing the game and be working in different parts of the game and they’re starting to give you feedback saying, hey, I don’t think this is working.

item.title: More Thoughts On Game Marketing
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to give more thoughts on game marketing and look, I exist in daytime.

Thought I’d make a change. Now, I should point out, I’ve already done a marketing video, which I’ll link below, but recently I was looking at it and I realized I did not follow my own rules on how to give constructive feedback, which I did in a video which I’ll also link below, where I said: if you’re going to give comments and critique on something, make it constructive, make it actionable. And I realized in that marketing video when I talked about that I’ve worked with marketers that I liked and marketers I didn’t like. I never really gave them constructive feedback. Now, nobody on the video who watched the video said: hey, Tim, you’re not following your own rules.

item.title: Do Devs Know What Gamers Want?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about do devs know what Gamers want.

This video is a direct followup to my video last week: do Gamers know what they like? Before I made that video I just mentioned, I was going to do it in a comment section almost two months ago and L Noir said personally, I think, do devs know what Gamers want? And is even a better question, and you know what they’re right. So both that video — so I made that video — and then I made this one pretty soon afterwards. Keep in mind these were made back in November, not even that late November, kind of mid November.

item.title: Do Gamers Know What They Like?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the question: do game players even know what they like?

I almost called this game players don’t know what they like, but I figured it would lead to a better comment section if I posed it as a question. Let me tell you why I thought of this.

I was watching a comedian podcast, not a comedy podcast. I wasn’t watching comedians do stand up. I was talking about comedians talk with each other about being Comedians and one of them made a comment that it’s very hard to come up with jokes in a world where audience only know what they find unfunny and most of them Couldn’t Write a joke if their lives depended on it, especially a joke that you could say in standup. And I thought that was interesting because, first of all, I’ve met a lot of people who think they’re funny and they are not funny. But also, standup comedy is a perfect example of it, doesn’t require any equipment or anything.

item.title: Feedback Needs Context
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how feedback needs context, because it’s hard to know what to do with feedback if you don’t have context.

In the absence of context, feedback is very difficult to do anything with. Now sidebar, I have done two videos on feedback: one on how to give good feedback and one on how to receive criticism, which just feedback. I will link those below. I am not going to repeat those videos here because there’s too much to go into and I would very much get sidetracked into the points I made in those videos. I highly recommend that you watch those two videos before watching this one so we don’t get into the Weeds on: well, what’s constructive feedback and what am I supposed to do with the feedback when I get it?

item.title: My Memoirs, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my Memoirs again, so consider this a my Memoirs video, part two.

So I’ve shown it before. It’s this big honken thing I wrote and got printed, sent to some people for feedback in that first video that I link below. You can watch it to learn why I wrote it, what’s inside and why I’m not going to publish it. So if you have questions about any of that, um, watch the first video, because I’m not going to talk about that here. But this Memoir and this channel has given me a lot of feedback on me and I’ve learned a lot, and these are things that are interesting and I’ll talk about a lot of them. But I want to really talk about the fact that, while I’ve gotten a lot of wonderful feedback, really nice things — especially some nice things from people I’ve worked with who could have said nothing that I haven’t talked to in 10, 15, in one case I heard from someone I haven’t talked to in, haven’t talked to, I haven’t talked to in 27 years, but they wanted to know that they liked working with me, which is really really sweet and really nice. But I’ve gotten a lot of negative feedback too, and I’ve gotten that negative feedback from people who meant it constructively and said: oh yeah, I loved working with You know you do this thing, though sometimes, and other people they were just angry and like, ah, you do this blah. But I heard it enough from both kinds of people that I’m like this stuff has got to be true. So let me tell you what the negative feedback I’ve gotten about me.

item.title: Editing Your Game Story
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Axion as a hypothetical particle to explain dark matter through their ability to violate CP symmetry in Quantum chromodynamics — wait, wrong Channel.

Today, I want to talk about editing your game story. I’m not a good writer — well, I’m not a writer, especially not a good one. However, I frequently find myself giving feedback to narrative designers because of my ability. My feedback doesn’t take the form of do this, I know better, but instead I give feedback in the form of: I know what you’re trying to do and it’s not coming through now. Sometimes I know what they’re trying to do because I’ve talked to them, or it’s one of the — uh, discussed pillars we had, for here’s what I want the characters to do. Sometimes it’s just I’m playing this character and I’m like this character seems to be trying to be my friend, but they’re just coming across as a sarcastic jerk that I don’t want anywhere near me.

item.title: "Git Gud"
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today let’s talk about the phrase get good.

I hate this phrase. Actually there’s a phrase I used to hate more, called n said um. People would frequently on the net, in Internet news groups, end a post with enough said, as if they had said everything that was possible to say on the possible that was possible to say on the topic and there was no need for a reply because it’s been exhausted. Um, and then if someone did reply, and then they replied, I’d often see people go. I thought you said enough was said. I thought you said you’d said enough. So I I think that’s why that one went away. But this get good phrase, well, let’s.

item.title: How To Receive Criticism
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how to receive criticism.

I basically want to talk to you about what you should do when people are being critical of your work or even your — opinions. Now, if you watched this Channel at all, you know that I do not think my games are perfect, far from it. I’m not just talking about the bugs that are in them — and there were some doozies, there were some, you know, pretty horrific crash bugs and slowdowns — but I’m talking about even the design bugs that were present in the game when they shipped, that I didn’t even like when they shipped. And I think I’ve talked about things, you know in Fallout and Arcanum that I didn’t like when they went out the door. Um, some of them we changed, like the timer and Fallout, and some of them I never got to change because they were never prioritized.

item.title: Giving Good Feedback
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer another question, this time from Brooke Richardson, 1373, who asks: can you do a video on giving good and relevant game design feedback, and how do we know when things aren’t balanced?

This is a good question because this is effectively what I do now as a semi-retired design consultant. What I’m effectively ask to do from companies that I work for is give them good and relevant design feedback. So I’ve talked a lot about making sure this is actionable. But let’s do a deep dive into exactly what I mean about how to create that feedback, how to deliver it, and then I’ll try to touch a bit more on the second question about balancing. So first, it’s really important, if you’re going to give feedback to someone about game, to know exactly what it is they’re making and know. That sounds dumb, but let me. Let me walk through what I mean by that, or it sounds obvious.
